2026-05-30 18:37:54,754 - INFO - swea-agent-prefix-dev__pixi-5850 - 💭 THOUGHT
Now let me understand the PR issue more clearly. The PR says:

> The `workspace.exclude-newer` overrides the channel version, which I believe to be wrong.
> It should be `workspace.exclude-newer` > `workspace.channels.exclude-newer` > `[(pypi-)exclude-newer]` IMO.

Wait, re-reading: "It should be `workspace.exclude-newer` > `workspace.channels.exclude-newer` > `[(pypi-)exclude-newer]` IMO."

This seems to say the order of precedence should be:
1. `workspace.exclude-newer` (LOWEST precedence, i.e., most general)
2. `workspace.channels.exclude-newer` (MIDDLE precedence)
3. `[(pypi-)exclude-newer]` (HIGHEST precedence, i.e., per-package overrides)

Wait, that doesn't match. Let me re-read: "The `workspace.exclude-newer` overrides the channel version, which I believe to be wrong."

So the bug is: when you set both `workspace.exclude-newer` and a channel-level `exclude-newer`, the workspace-level one takes precedence (overrides) the channel-level one. The PR author says it should be the opposite: channel-level should take precedence over workspace-level.

But looking at the expected behavior: "It should be `workspace.exclude-newer` > `workspace.channels.exclude-newer` > `[(pypi-)exclude-newer]` IMO."

Hmm, the `>` notation could mean precedence (higher on left wins) or could mean "greater" in scope. Let me look at the example from the PR:

```toml
[workspace]
channels = [
    { channel = "conda-forge", exclude-newer = "3y"},
    { channel = "bioconda", exclude-newer = "1y"},
    { channel = "https://prefix.dev/github-releases", exclude-newer = "2d"}] # doesn't work
platforms = ["osx-arm64", "linux-64", "linux-aarch64", "osx-64", "win-64"]
# Takes precedence over the channel-level setting
exclude-newer = "1y"

[dependencies]
python = "*"
chainsaw = "*" # from github-releases
```

The issue is that `chainsaw` from `github-releases` channel has a channel-level `exclude-newer = "2d"` which should allow packages from the last 2 days, but the workspace-level `exclude-newer = "1y"` overrides it, making it 1 year. The author expects the channel-level setting to take precedence over the workspace-level setting.

But wait, "2d" is more restrictive than "1y". If `exclude-newer = "2d"` means "exclude packages newer than 2 days ago", and `exclude-newer = "1y"` means "exclude packages newer than 1 year ago", then the workspace-level `1y` is actually LESS restrictive (allows more packages). But the error says:

```
chainsaw * cannot be installed because there are no viable options:
   └─ chainsaw 0.2.5 | ... is excluded because the package is uploaded after the cutoff date of 2025-04-08 04:56:48.943953
```

The cutoff date is `2025-04-08`, which is about 1 year from now (assuming now is ~2025). So the workspace-level `1y` is being used instead of the channel-level `2d`.

Now let me trace through the code in `exclude_newer_config_resolved`:

```rust
fn exclude_newer_config_resolved(
    &self,
) -> Result<Option<ResolvedExcludeNewer>, ParseChannelError> {
    let mut exclude_newer = self
        .exclude_newer_raw()  // workspace.exclude_newer = "1y"
        .map(|config| ResolvedExcludeNewer::from_datetime(config.cutoff()));  // Some with cutoff = now - 1y
    
    for channel in self.prioritized_channels().into_values() {
        let Some(channel_exclude_newer) = channel.exclude_newer else {
            continue;
        };
        
        let config = exclude_newer.get_or_insert_with(|| {
            ResolvedExcludeNewer::from_datetime(DateTime::<Utc>::MAX_UTC)
        });
        
        *config = match channel_exclude_newer {
            ExcludeNewer::Timestamp(dt) => config
                .clone()
                .with_channel_cutoff(channel.channel.to_string(), dt),
            ExcludeNewer::Duration(duration) => config.clone().with_channel_cutoff(
                channel.channel.to_string(),
                ExcludeNewer::Duration(duration).cutoff(),
            ),
        };
    }
    // ... package overrides ...
}
```

So `exclude_newer` starts as `Some(ResolvedExcludeNewer { cutoff: now - 1y, ... })`. Then for the `github-releases` channel, it adds a channel cutoff: `channel_cutoffs["https://prefix.dev/github-releases"] = now - 2d`.

The resulting config should have:
- `cutoff = now - 1y` (default)
- `channel_cutoffs = {"https://prefix.dev/github-releases": now - 2d}` (channel override)

Now, when checking if a package is excluded, the code should use `cutoff_for_package(package_name, channel)`, which looks at channel_cutoffs first, then falls back to the default cutoff.

But wait, looking at the error message: "the cutoff date of 2025-04-08". Let me check the `ResolvedExcludeNewer` conversion to `rattler_solve::ExcludeNewer`:

```rust
impl From<ResolvedExcludeNewer> for rattler_solve::ExcludeNewer {
    fn from(value: ResolvedExcludeNewer) -> Self {
        let mut config = rattler_solve::ExcludeNewer::from_datetime(value.cutoff)
            .with_include_unknown_timestamp(value.include_unknown_timestamp);
        for (channel, cutoff) in value.channel_cutoffs {
            config = config.with_channel_cutoff(channel, cutoff);
        }
        for (package, cutoff) in value.package_cutoffs {
            config = config.with_package_cutoff(package, cutoff);
        }
        config
    }
}
```

This looks correct - it passes the channel cutoffs through. So the issue might be in how `rattler_solve::ExcludeNewer` handles the `is_excluded` check, or in how the channels are being matched (maybe the channel name doesn't match?).

     1	//! `rattler_solve` is a crate that provides functionality to solve Conda
     2	//! environments. It currently exposes the functionality through the
     3	//! [`SolverImpl::solve`] function.
     4	
     5	#![deny(missing_docs)]
     6	
     7	#[cfg(feature = "libsolv_c")]
     8	pub mod libsolv_c;
     9	#[cfg(feature = "resolvo")]
    10	pub mod resolvo;
    11	
    12	use std::collections::HashMap;
    13	use std::fmt;
    14	
    15	use chrono::{DateTime, Utc};
    16	use rattler_conda_types::{
    17	    utils::TimestampMs, GenericVirtualPackage, MatchSpec, PackageName, RepoDataRecord, SolverResult,
    18	};
    19	
    20	/// Represents a solver implementation, capable of solving [`SolverTask`]s
    21	pub trait SolverImpl {
    22	    /// The repo data associated to a channel and platform combination
    23	    type RepoData<'a>: SolverRepoData<'a>;
    24	
    25	    /// Resolve the dependencies and return the [`RepoDataRecord`]s that should
    26	    /// be present in the environment.
    27	    fn solve<
    28	        'a,
    29	        R: IntoRepoData<'a, Self::RepoData<'a>>,
    30	        TAvailablePackagesIterator: IntoIterator<Item = R>,
    31	    >(
    32	        &mut self,
    33	        task: SolverTask<TAvailablePackagesIterator>,
    34	    ) -> Result<SolverResult, SolveError>;
    35	}
    36	
    37	/// Represents an error when solving the dependencies for a given environment
    38	#[derive(thiserror::Error, Debug)]
    39	pub enum SolveError {
    40	    /// There is no set of dependencies that satisfies the requirements
    41	    Unsolvable(Vec<String>),
    42	
    43	    /// The solver backend returned operations that we dont know how to install.
    44	    /// Each string is a somewhat user-friendly representation of which
    45	    /// operation was not recognized and can be used for error reporting
    46	    UnsupportedOperations(Vec<String>),
    47	
    48	    /// Error when converting matchspec
    49	    #[error(transparent)]
    50	    ParseMatchSpecError(#[from] rattler_conda_types::ParseMatchSpecError),
    51	
    52	    /// Encountered duplicate records in the available packages.
    53	    DuplicateRecords(String),
    54	
    55	    /// To support Resolvo cancellation
    56	    Cancelled,
    57	}
    58	
    59	impl fmt::Display for SolveError {
    60	    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
    61	        match self {
    62	            SolveError::Unsolvable(operations) => {
    63	                write!(
    64	                    f,
    65	                    "Cannot solve the request because of: {}",
    66	                    operations.join(", ")
    67	                )
    68	            }
    69	            SolveError::UnsupportedOperations(operations) => {
    70	                write!(f, "Unsupported operations: {}", operations.join(", "))
    71	            }
    72	            SolveError::ParseMatchSpecError(e) => {
    73	                write!(f, "Error parsing match spec: {e}")
    74	            }
    75	            SolveError::Cancelled => {
    76	                write!(f, "Solve operation has been cancelled")
    77	            }
    78	            SolveError::DuplicateRecords(filename) => {
    79	                write!(f, "encountered duplicate records for {filename}")
    80	            }
    81	        }
    82	    }
    83	}
    84	
    85	/// Configuration for filtering packages newer than a cutoff.
    86	///
    87	/// This feature helps reduce the risk of installing compromised packages by
    88	/// delaying the installation of newly published versions. In most cases,
    89	/// malicious releases are discovered and removed from channels within a short
    90	/// time window (often within an hour). By requiring packages to have been
    91	/// published for a minimum duration, you give the community time to identify
    92	/// and report malicious packages before they can be installed.
    93	///
    94	/// This is similar to pnpm's `minimumReleaseAge` feature.
    95	///
    96	/// # Example
    97	///
    98	/// ```
    99	/// use std::time::Duration;
   100	/// use rattler_solve::ExcludeNewer;
   101	///
   102	/// // Only allow packages that have been published for at least 1 hour
   103	/// let config = ExcludeNewer::from_duration(Duration::from_secs(60 * 60))
   104	///     // But allow "my-internal-package" to use a package-specific cutoff
   105	///     .with_package_duration("my-internal-package".parse().unwrap(), Duration::ZERO)
   106	///     // And allow a trusted internal channel to skip the delay entirely
   107	///     .with_channel_duration("my-internal-channel", Duration::ZERO);
   108	/// ```
   109	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
   110	pub struct ExcludeNewer {
   111	    /// The default cutoff date. Packages uploaded after this date are excluded.
   112	    cutoff: DateTime<Utc>,
   113	
   114	    /// Channel-specific cutoff dates that override [`Self::cutoff`] for
   115	    /// records from matching channels.
   116	    ///
   117	    /// The key is matched against [`RepoDataRecord::channel`] exactly.
   118	    channel_cutoffs: HashMap<String, DateTime<Utc>>,
   119	
   120	    /// Package-specific cutoff dates that override both [`Self::cutoff`] and
   121	    /// [`Self::channel_cutoffs`] for matching package names.
   122	    package_cutoffs: HashMap<PackageName, DateTime<Utc>>,
   123	
   124	    /// Whether to include packages that don't have a timestamp.
   125	    include_unknown_timestamp: bool,
   126	}
   127	
   128	impl ExcludeNewer {
   129	    fn cutoff_from_duration(duration: std::time::Duration, now: DateTime<Utc>) -> DateTime<Utc> {
   130	        let duration =
   131	            chrono::Duration::from_std(duration).expect("exclude_newer duration is too large");
   132	        now - duration
   133	    }
   134	
   135	    /// Creates a new configuration from an absolute cutoff date.
   136	    pub fn from_datetime(cutoff: DateTime<Utc>) -> Self {
   137	        Self {
   138	            cutoff,
   139	            channel_cutoffs: HashMap::new(),
   140	            package_cutoffs: HashMap::new(),
   141	            include_unknown_timestamp: false,
   142	        }
   143	    }
   144	
   145	    /// Creates a new configuration from a relative duration.
   146	    pub fn from_duration(duration: std::time::Duration) -> Self {
   147	        Self::from_duration_with_now(duration, Utc::now())
   148	    }
   149	
   150	    /// Creates a new configuration from a relative duration and explicit
   151	    /// reference time.
   152	    pub fn from_duration_with_now(duration: std::time::Duration, now: DateTime<Utc>) -> Self {
   153	        Self {
   154	            cutoff: Self::cutoff_from_duration(duration, now),
   155	            channel_cutoffs: HashMap::new(),
   156	            package_cutoffs: HashMap::new(),
   157	            include_unknown_timestamp: false,
   158	        }
   159	    }
   160	
   161	    /// Sets the absolute cutoff override for a specific package.
   162	    pub fn with_package_cutoff(mut self, package: PackageName, cutoff: DateTime<Utc>) -> Self {
   163	        self.package_cutoffs.insert(package, cutoff);
   164	        self
   165	    }
   166	
   167	    /// Sets the duration override for a specific package.
   168	    pub fn with_package_duration(
   169	        mut self,
   170	        package: PackageName,
   171	        duration: std::time::Duration,
   172	    ) -> Self {
   173	        self.package_cutoffs
   174	            .insert(package, Self::cutoff_from_duration(duration, Utc::now()));
   175	        self
   176	    }
   177	
   178	    /// Sets the duration override for a specific package using an explicit
   179	    /// reference time.
   180	    pub fn with_package_duration_with_now(
   181	        mut self,
   182	        package: PackageName,
   183	        duration: std::time::Duration,
   184	        now: DateTime<Utc>,
   185	    ) -> Self {
   186	        self.package_cutoffs
   187	            .insert(package, Self::cutoff_from_duration(duration, now));
   188	        self
   189	    }
   190	
   191	    /// Sets the duration override for a specific channel.
   192	    pub fn with_channel_duration(
   193	        mut self,
   194	        channel: impl Into<String>,
   195	        duration: std::time::Duration,
   196	    ) -> Self {
   197	        self.channel_cutoffs.insert(
   198	            channel.into(),
   199	            Self::cutoff_from_duration(duration, Utc::now()),
   200	        );
   201	        self
   202	    }
   203	
   204	    /// Sets the duration override for a specific channel using an explicit
   205	    /// reference time.
   206	    pub fn with_channel_duration_with_now(
   207	        mut self,
   208	        channel: impl Into<String>,
   209	        duration: std::time::Duration,
   210	        now: DateTime<Utc>,
   211	    ) -> Self {
   212	        self.channel_cutoffs
   213	            .insert(channel.into(), Self::cutoff_from_duration(duration, now));
   214	        self
   215	    }
   216	
   217	    /// Sets the absolute cutoff override for a specific channel.
   218	    pub fn with_channel_cutoff(
   219	        mut self,
   220	        channel: impl Into<String>,
   221	        cutoff: DateTime<Utc>,
   222	    ) -> Self {
   223	        self.channel_cutoffs.insert(channel.into(), cutoff);
   224	        self
   225	    }
   226	
   227	    /// Sets whether packages without a timestamp should be included.
   228	    ///
   229	    /// Call this to override the constructor default.
   230	    pub fn with_include_unknown_timestamp(mut self, include: bool) -> Self {
   231	        self.include_unknown_timestamp = include;
   232	        self
   233	    }
   234	
   235	    /// Returns whether packages without a timestamp are included.
   236	    pub fn include_unknown_timestamp(&self) -> bool {
   237	        self.include_unknown_timestamp
   238	    }
   239	
   240	    /// Computes the cutoff time for the given package and channel.
   241	    pub fn cutoff_for_package(
   242	        &self,
   243	        package: &PackageName,
   244	        channel: Option<&str>,
   245	    ) -> DateTime<Utc> {
   246	        self.package_cutoffs
   247	            .get(package)
   248	            .copied()
   249	            .or_else(|| channel.and_then(|channel| self.channel_cutoffs.get(channel).copied()))
   250	            .unwrap_or(self.cutoff)
   251	    }
   252	
   253	    /// Returns whether a package should be excluded.
   254	    pub fn is_excluded(
   255	        &self,
   256	        package: &PackageName,
   257	        channel: Option<&str>,
   258	        timestamp: Option<&TimestampMs>,
   259	    ) -> bool {
   260	        match timestamp {
   261	            Some(timestamp) => *timestamp > self.cutoff_for_package(package, channel),
   262	            None => !self.include_unknown_timestamp(),
   263	        }
   264	    }
   265	}
   266	
   267	impl From<DateTime<Utc>> for ExcludeNewer {
   268	    fn from(value: DateTime<Utc>) -> Self {
   269	        Self::from_datetime(value)
   270	    }
   271	}
   272	
   273	impl From<std::time::Duration> for ExcludeNewer {
   274	    fn from(value: std::time::Duration) -> Self {
   275	        Self::from_duration(value)
   276	    }
   277	}
   278	
   279	/// Represents the channel priority option to use during solves.
   280	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Default)]
   281	#[cfg_attr(feature = "serde", derive(serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize))]
   282	#[cfg_attr(feature = "serde", serde(rename_all = "kebab-case"))]
   283	pub enum ChannelPriority {
   284	    /// The channel that the package is first found in will be used as the only
   285	    /// channel for that package.
   286	    #[default]
   287	    Strict,
   288	
   289	    // Conda also has "Flexible" as an option, where packages present in multiple channels
   290	    // are only taken from lower-priority channels when this prevents unsatisfiable environment
   291	    // errors, but this would need implementation in the solvers.
   292	    // Flexible,
   293	    /// Packages can be retrieved from any channel as package version takes
   294	    /// precedence.
   295	    Disabled,
   296	}
   297	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
   298	/// Represents a dependency resolution task, to be solved by one of the backends
   299	pub struct SolverTask<TAvailablePackagesIterator> {
   300	    /// An iterator over all available packages

2026-05-30 18:38:34,777 - INFO - swea-agent-prefix-dev__pixi-5850 - 💭 THOUGHT
Now I see the `cutoff_for_package` method clearly:

```rust
pub fn cutoff_for_package(
    &self,
    package: &PackageName,
    channel: Option<&str>,
) -> DateTime<Utc> {
    self.package_cutoffs
        .get(package)
        .copied()
        .or_else(|| channel.and_then(|channel| self.channel_cutoffs.get(channel).copied()))
        .unwrap_or(self.cutoff)
}
```

So the precedence is:
1. Package-level cutoffs (highest priority)
2. Channel-level cutoffs
3. Default cutoff

That matches the expected behavior. So the issue is not in `rattler_solve::ExcludeNewer` but somewhere in how the config is being built or how the channel is being matched.

   360	            // runs.
   361	            let mut ordered_repodata = Vec::with_capacity(repo_data.records.len());
   362	            let mut package_to_type: HashMap<&ArchiveIdentifier, (DistArchiveType, usize, bool)> =
   363	                HashMap::with_capacity(repo_data.records.len());
   364	
   365	            for record in repo_data.records {
   366	                // Determine if this record will be excluded by exclude_newer.
   367	                let excluded = exclude_newer.as_ref().is_some_and(|config| {
   368	                    config.is_excluded(
   369	                        &record.package_record.name,
   370	                        record.channel.as_deref(),
   371	                        record.package_record.timestamp.as_ref(),
   372	                    )
   373	                });
   374	
   375	                let identifier = &record.identifier.identifier;
   376	                let archive_type = record.identifier.archive_type;
   377	                match package_to_type.get_mut(identifier) {
   378	                    None => {
   379	                        let idx = ordered_repodata.len();
   380	                        ordered_repodata.push(record);
   381	                        package_to_type.insert(identifier, (archive_type, idx, excluded));
   382	                    }
   383	                    Some((prev_archive_type, idx, previous_excluded)) => {
   384	                        if *previous_excluded && !excluded {
   385	                            // The previous package would have been excluded by the solver. If the
   386	                            // current record won't be excluded we should always use that.
   387	                            *prev_archive_type = archive_type;
   388	                            ordered_repodata[*idx] = record;
   389	                            *previous_excluded = false;
   390	                        } else if excluded && !*previous_excluded {
   391	                            // The previous package would not have been excluded
   392	                            // by the solver but
   393	                            // this one will, so we'll keep the previous one
   394	                            // regardless of the type.
   395	                        } else {
   396	                            match archive_type.cmp_preference(*prev_archive_type) {
   397	                                Ordering::Greater => {
   398	                                    // A previous package has a worse package "type", we'll use the
   399	                                    // current record instead.
   400	                                    *prev_archive_type = archive_type;
   401	                                    ordered_repodata[*idx] = record;
   402	                                    *previous_excluded = excluded;
   403	                                }
   404	                                Ordering::Less => {
   405	                                    // A previous package that we already stored
   406	                                    // is actually a package of a better
   407	                                    // "type" so we'll just use that instead
   408	                                    // (.conda > .tar.bz)
   409	                                }
   410	                                Ordering::Equal => {
   411	                                    return Err(SolveError::DuplicateRecords(
   412	                                        record.identifier.to_string(),
   413	                                    ));
   414	                                }
   415	                            }
   416	                        }
   417	                    }
   418	                }
   419	            }
   420	
   421	            for record in ordered_repodata {
   422	                let package_name = pool.intern_package_name(&record.package_record.name);
   423	                let solvable_id =
   424	                    pool.intern_solvable(package_name, SolverPackageRecord::Record(record));
   425	
   426	                // Update records with all entries in a single mutable borrow
   427	                let candidates = records.entry(package_name).or_default();
   428	                candidates.candidates.push(solvable_id);
   429	
   430	                if let Some(config) = &exclude_newer {
   431	                    if config.is_excluded(
   432	                        &record.package_record.name,
   433	                        record.channel.as_deref(),
   434	                        record.package_record.timestamp.as_ref(),
   435	                    ) {
   436	                        let reason = pool.intern_string(
   437	                            exclude_newer_reason(

   439	                                &record.package_record.name,
   440	                                record.channel.as_deref(),

    80	///
    81	/// Panics if the repo does not belong to the pool
    82	pub fn add_repodata_records<'a>(
    83	    pool: &Pool,
    84	    repo: &Repo<'_>,
    85	    repo_data: impl IntoIterator<Item = &'a RepoDataRecord>,
    86	    exclude_newer: Option<&ExcludeNewer>,
    87	) -> Result<Vec<SolvableId>, SolveError> {
    88	    // Sanity check
    89	    repo.ensure_belongs_to_pool(pool);
    90	
    91	    // Get all the IDs (these strings are internal to libsolv and always present, so
    92	    // we can unwrap them at will)
    93	    let solvable_buildflavor_id = pool.find_interned_str(SOLVABLE_BUILDFLAVOR).unwrap();
    94	    let solvable_buildtime_id = pool.find_interned_str(SOLVABLE_BUILDTIME).unwrap();
    95	    let solvable_buildversion_id = pool.find_interned_str(SOLVABLE_BUILDVERSION).unwrap();
    96	    let solvable_constraints = pool.find_interned_str(SOLVABLE_CONSTRAINS).unwrap();

    98	    let solvable_license_id = pool.find_interned_str(SOLVABLE_LICENSE).unwrap();
    99	    let solvable_pkg_id = pool.find_interned_str(SOLVABLE_PKGID).unwrap();
   100	    let solvable_checksum = pool.find_interned_str(SOLVABLE_CHECKSUM).unwrap();
   101	    let solvable_track_features = pool.find_interned_str(SOLVABLE_TRACK_FEATURES).unwrap();
   102	    let repo_type_md5 = pool.find_interned_str(REPOKEY_TYPE_MD5).unwrap();
   103	    let repo_type_sha256 = pool.find_interned_str(REPOKEY_TYPE_SHA256).unwrap();
   104	
   105	    // Custom id
   106	    let solvable_index_id = pool.intern_str(SOLVABLE_REPODATA_RECORD_INDEX);
   107	
   108	    // Keeps a mapping from packages added to the repo to the type and solvable
   109	    let mut package_to_type: HashMap<&ArchiveIdentifier, (DistArchiveType, SolvableId)> =
   110	        HashMap::new();
   111	
   112	    // Through `data` we can manipulate solvables (see the `Repodata` docs for
   113	    // details)
   114	    let data = repo.add_repodata();
   115	
   116	    let mut solvable_ids = Vec::new();
   117	
   118	    // Track all extras we encounter so we can create synthetic solvables for them
   119	    let mut extras: HashSet<(String, String)> = HashSet::new();
   120	    for (repo_data_index, repo_data) in repo_data.into_iter().enumerate() {
   121	        if let Some(config) = exclude_newer {
   122	            if config.is_excluded(
   123	                &repo_data.package_record.name,
   124	                repo_data.channel.as_deref(),
   125	                repo_data.package_record.timestamp.as_ref(),
   126	            ) {
   127	                continue;
   128	            }
   129	        }
   130	
   131	        // Create a solvable for the package
   132	        let solvable_id =
   133	            match add_or_reuse_solvable(pool, repo, &data, &mut package_to_type, repo_data)? {
   134	                Some(id) => id,
   135	                None => continue,
   136	            };
   137	
   138	        // Store the current index so we can retrieve the original repo data record
   139	        // from the final transaction
   140	        data.set_num(solvable_id, solvable_index_id, repo_data_index as u64);
   141	
   142	        // Safe because there are no other active references to any solvable (so no
   143	        // aliasing)
   144	        let solvable = unsafe { solvable_id.resolve_raw(pool).as_mut() };

   146	
   147	        // Name and version
   148	        solvable.name = pool.intern_str(record.name.as_normalized()).into();

   157	    type Err = url::ParseError;
   158	
   159	    fn from_str(s: &str) -> Result<Self, Self::Err> {
   160	        if parse_scheme(s).is_some() {
   161	            Ok(NamedChannelOrUrl::Url(Url::from_str(s)?))
   162	        } else if is_path(s) {
   163	            Ok(NamedChannelOrUrl::Path(s.into()))
   164	        } else {
   165	            Ok(NamedChannelOrUrl::Name(s.to_string()))
   166	        }
   167	    }

I see that when constructing `RepoDataRecord` manually, the channel is often set to `None`. But for the actual channel repodata, it's fetched from remote. Let me check how the gateway fetches repo data and assigns the channel field.

Actually, let me step back and think about this differently. The issue in the PR description is that `workspace.exclude-newer` overrides the channel version. Let me re-read the code in `features_ext.rs`:

```rust
fn exclude_newer_config_resolved(
    &self,
) -> Result<Option<ResolvedExcludeNewer>, ParseChannelError> {
    let mut exclude_newer = self
        .exclude_newer_raw()
        .map(|config| ResolvedExcludeNewer::from_datetime(config.cutoff()));
    
    for channel in self.prioritized_channels().into_values() {
        let Some(channel_exclude_newer) = channel.exclude_newer else {
            continue;
        };
        
        let config = exclude_newer.get_or_insert_with(|| {
            ResolvedExcludeNewer::from_datetime(DateTime::<Utc>::MAX_UTC)
        });
        
        *config = match channel_exclude_newer {
            ExcludeNewer::Timestamp(dt) => config
                .clone()
                .with_channel_cutoff(channel.channel.to_string(), dt),
            ExcludeNewer::Duration(duration) => config.clone().with_channel_cutoff(
                channel.channel.to_string(),
                ExcludeNewer::Duration(duration).cutoff(),
            ),
        };
    }
    // ... package overrides
}
```

Wait, I see a potential issue here! On line 140-148, the code does:

```rust
*config = match channel_exclude_newer {
    ExcludeNewer::Timestamp(dt) => config
        .clone()
        .with_channel_cutoff(channel.channel.to_string(), dt),
    ExcludeNewer::Duration(duration) => config.clone().with_channel_cutoff(
        channel.channel.to_string(),
        ExcludeNewer::Duration(duration).cutoff(),
    ),
};
```

This creates a new `ResolvedExcludeNewer` with the channel cutoff added. But notice that `config.clone()` preserves the existing cutoff (which comes from `workspace.exclude-newer`). The channel cutoff is *added* to the existing config, not *replacing* the base cutoff. This is correct behavior - the channel-level setting should override the workspace-level for that specific channel.

But wait, looking at line 136-138:
```rust
let config = exclude_newer.get_or_insert_with(|| {
    ResolvedExcludeNewer::from_datetime(DateTime::<Utc>::MAX_UTC)
});
```

If `exclude_newer` is already `Some` (because workspace-level is set), this just gets the existing config. Then the channel cutoff is added on top. This seems correct!

But what if there's NO workspace-level exclude_newer? Then `exclude_newer` starts as `None`. The first channel with `exclude_newer` triggers `get_or_insert_with` which creates a config with `DateTime::<Utc>::MAX_UTC` as the default cutoff. Then the channel's cutoff is added as a channel-specific override. 

Hmm, but then for channels WITHOUT a channel-level exclude_newer, the cutoff would be `DateTime::<Utc>::MAX_UTC` (i.e., no packages excluded) which is correct.

So the logic seems correct at this level. Let me check if the issue might be somewhere else.

Let me look at the PR description again more carefully:

```toml
[workspace]
channels = [
    { channel = "conda-forge", exclude-newer = "3y"},
    { channel = "bioconda", exclude-newer = "1y"},
    { channel = "https://prefix.dev/github-releases", exclude-newer = "2d"}] # doesn't work
platforms = ["osx-arm64", "linux-64", "linux-aarch64", "osx-64", "win-64"]
# Takes precedence over the channel-level setting
exclude-newer = "1y"
```

The workspace has `exclude-newer = "1y"` and the channel `https://prefix.dev/github-releases` has `exclude-newer = "2d"`. The package `chainsaw` is only on `github-releases`. The expected behavior is that `chainsaw` should be allowed if it was uploaded within the last 2 days (channel-level setting `2d`), but instead it's being excluded based on the workspace-level `1y`.

Wait, actually `1y` means "exclude packages newer than 1 year ago" while `2d` means "exclude packages newer than 2 days ago." So `2d` is MORE restrictive, not less. If the channel-level `2d` was being applied, packages would need to be at least 2 days old. If the workspace-level `1y` is being applied, packages need to be at least 1 year old. The error says cutoff date is `2025-04-08` which is roughly 1 year ago from now (April 2026). So it seems like the workspace-level `1y` is being used.

So the issue is that the workspace-level `exclude-newer` is being used instead of the channel-level one. But based on the code analysis, the channel-level cutoff should override the workspace-level default for that specific channel.

Unless... there's a bug in how the channel name is matched. Let me check: the channel is `"https://prefix.dev/github-releases"` (a URL). The repo data record's channel field might be something like `"https://prefix.dev/github-releases"` or it might be different. Let me check what the gateway sets for the channel.

Actually, wait. Let me look at this more carefully. The PR says the issue is on "current unreleased main." Let me check if there have been any recent changes to this code. But first, let me look at the `pypi_exclude_newer_config_resolved` function for comparison:

```rust
fn pypi_exclude_newer_config_resolved(&self) -> ResolvedPypiExcludeNewer {
    let mut exclude_newer = self
        .exclude_newer_raw()
        .map(|config| ResolvedPypiExcludeNewer::from_datetime(config.cutoff()))
        .unwrap_or_default();
    
    for (name, package_exclude_newer) in &self
        .workspace_manifest()
        .workspace
        .pypi_exclude_newer_package_overrides
    {
        // ...
    }
    
    exclude_newer
}
```

Notice that the PyPI version does NOT apply channel overrides at all! It only applies package overrides. That's a separate issue.

Now, looking back at the `exclude_newer_config_resolved`, I see that when there's a workspace-level `exclude_newer`:

1. `exclude_newer = Some(ResolvedExcludeNewer { cutoff: "now - 1y", ... })`
2. For each channel with its own `exclude_newer`, it adds a channel-specific cutoff

So the result should be:
- Default cutoff: `now - 1y` (from workspace)
- Channel cutoff for `https://prefix.dev/github-releases`: `now - 2d` (from channel)

When checking a package from `https://prefix.dev/github-releases`, the solver would use `cutoff_for_package(package, Some("https://prefix.dev/github-releases"))`, which should return `now - 2d`. That's MORE restrictive than `now - 1y`.

But wait, the error message shows: "the cutoff date of 2025-04-08 04:56:48.943953". If today is April 2026, then `now - 1y` would be around April 2025. That matches! So the workspace-level `1y` IS being applied instead of the channel-level `2d`.

Unless... maybe the channel string in the repo data doesn't match the channel string used as the key in `channel_cutoffs`?

Let me check if there's any mismatch. The channel in the config is `"https://prefix.dev/github-releases"`. The `NamedChannelOrUrl::to_string()` for a URL returns the URL as-is. So the key in `channel_cutoffs` would be `"https://prefix.dev/github-releases"`. 

But what does the `RepoDataRecord::channel` contain? It might be a normalized form. Let me check how the gateway fetches repo data:

    34	/// Additional knobs that allow you to tweak the behavior of
    35	/// [`fetch_repo_data`].
    36	#[derive(Clone)]
    37	pub struct FetchRepoDataOptions {
    38	    /// How to use the cache. By default it will cache and reuse downloaded
    39	    /// repodata.json (if the server allows it).
    40	    pub cache_action: CacheAction,
    41	
    42	    /// Determines which variant to download. See [`Variant`] for more
    43	    /// information.
    44	    pub variant: Variant,
    45	
    46	    /// When enabled, the zstd variant will be used if available
    47	    pub zstd_enabled: bool,
    48	
    49	    /// When enabled, the bz2 variant will be used if available
    50	    pub bz2_enabled: bool,
    51	
    52	    /// Retry policy to use when streaming the response is interrupted. If this
    53	    /// is `None` the default retry policy is used.
    54	    pub retry_policy: Option<Arc<dyn RetryPolicy + Send + Sync>>,
    55	}
    56	
    57	impl Default for FetchRepoDataOptions {
    58	    fn default() -> Self {
    59	        Self {
    60	            cache_action: CacheAction::default(),
    61	            variant: Variant::default(),
    62	            zstd_enabled: true,
    63	            bz2_enabled: true,
    64	            retry_policy: None,
    65	        }
    66	    }
    67	}
    68	
    69	/// The result of [`fetch_repo_data`].
    70	#[derive(Debug)]
    71	pub struct CachedRepoData {
    72	    /// A lockfile that guards access to any of the repodata.json file or its
    73	    /// cache.
    74	    pub lock_file: LockedFile,
    75	
    76	    /// The path to the uncompressed repodata.json file.
    77	    pub repo_data_json_path: PathBuf,
    78	
    79	    /// The cache data.
    80	    pub cache_state: RepoDataState,
    81	
    82	    /// How the cache was used for this request.
    83	    pub cache_result: CacheResult,
    84	}
    85

    87	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]

    89	    /// The cache was hit, the data on disk was already valid.
    90	    CacheHit,
    91	
    92	    /// The cache was hit, we did have to check with the server, but no data was

    94	    CacheHitAfterFetch,
    95

    88	
    89	enum SparseRepoDataInner {
    90	    /// The repo data is stored as a memory mapped file
    91	    #[cfg(any(unix, windows))]
    92	    Memmapped(MemmappedSparseRepoDataInner),
    93	    /// The repo data is stored as `Bytes`
    94	    Bytes(BytesSparseRepoDataInner),
    95	}
    96	
    97	impl SparseRepoDataInner {
    98	    fn borrow_repo_data(&self) -> &LazyRepoData<'_> {
    99	        match self {
   100	            #[cfg(any(unix, windows))]
   101	            SparseRepoDataInner::Memmapped(inner) => inner.borrow_dependent(),
   102	            SparseRepoDataInner::Bytes(inner) => inner.borrow_dependent(),
   103	        }
   104	    }
   105	}
   106	
   107	// A struct that holds a memory map of a `repodata.json` file and also a
   108	// self-referential field which indexes the data in the memory map with a
   109	// sparsely parsed json struct. See [`LazyRepoData`].
   110	#[cfg(any(unix, windows))]
   111	self_cell::self_cell!(
   112	    struct MemmappedSparseRepoDataInner {
   113	        // Memory map of the `repodata.json` file
   114	        owner: memmap2::Mmap,
   115	
   116	        // Sparsely parsed json content of the memory map. This data struct holds
   117	        // references into the memory map so we have to use ouroboros to make
   118	        // this legal.
   119	        #[covariant]
   120	        dependent: LazyRepoData,
   121	    }
   122	);
   123	
   124	// A struct that holds a reference to the bytes of a `repodata.json` file and
   125	// also a self-referential field which indexes the data in the `bytes` with a
   126	// sparsely parsed json struct. See [`LazyRepoData`].
   127	self_cell::self_cell!(
   128	    struct BytesSparseRepoDataInner {
   129	        // Bytes of the `repodata.json` file
   130	        owner: Bytes,
   131	
   132	        // Sparsely parsed json content of the file's bytes. This data struct holds
   133	        // references into the bytes so we have to use ouroboros to make this
   134	        // legal.
   135	        #[covariant]
   136	        dependent: LazyRepoData,
   137	    }
   138	);
   139	
   140	impl SparseRepoData {
   141	    /// Construct an instance of self from a file on disk and a [`Channel`].
   142	    ///
   143	    /// The `patch_function` can be used to patch the package record after it
   144	    /// has been parsed (e.g. to add `pip` to `python`).
   145	    ///
   146	    /// On Windows the file is opened with `FILE_SHARE_DELETE` so that another
   147	    /// process/thread can rename or delete the file while it is still mapped.
   148	    #[cfg(any(unix, windows))]
   149	    pub fn from_file(
   150	        channel: Channel,
   151	        subdir: impl Into<String>,
   152	        path: impl AsRef<Path>,
   153	        patch_function: Option<fn(&mut PackageRecord)>,
   154	    ) -> Result<Self, io::Error> {
   155	        #[cfg(windows)]
   156	        let file = {
   157	            use std::os::windows::fs::OpenOptionsExt;
   158	            const SHARE_ALL: u32 = 0x01 | 0x02 | 0x04; // FILE_SHARE_READ | WRITE | DELETE
   159	            std::fs::OpenOptions::new()
   160	                .read(true)
   161	                .share_mode(SHARE_ALL)
   162	                .open(path.as_ref())?
   163	        };
   164	        #[cfg(not(windows))]
   165	        let file = std::fs::File::from(fs_err::File::open(path.as_ref())?);
   166	
   167	        let memory_map = unsafe { memmap2::Mmap::map(&file) }?;
   168	        Ok(SparseRepoData {
   169	            inner: SparseRepoDataInner::Memmapped(MemmappedSparseRepoDataInner::try_new(
   170	                memory_map,
   171	                |memory_map| serde_json::from_slice(memory_map.as_ref()),
   172	            )?),
   173	            subdir: subdir.into(),
   174	            channel,
   175	            patch_record_fn: patch_function,
   176	        })
   177	    }
   178	
   179	    /// Construct an instance of self from a file on disk and a [`Channel`].
   180	    ///
   181	    /// The `patch_function` can be used to patch the package record after it
   182	    /// has been parsed (e.g. to add `pip` to `python`).
   183	    #[cfg(not(any(windows, unix)))]
   184	    pub fn from_file(
   185	        channel: Channel,
   186	        subdir: impl Into<String>,
   187	        path: impl AsRef<Path>,
   188	        patch_function: Option<fn(&mut PackageRecord)>,
   189	    ) -> Result<Self, io::Error> {
   190	        let bytes = fs_err::read(path)?;
   191	        Ok(Self::from_bytes(
   192	            channel,
   193	            subdir,
   194	            bytes.into(),
   195	            patch_function,
   196	        )?)
   197	    }
   198	
   199	    /// Construct an instance of self from a bytes and a [`Channel`].
   200	    ///

   376	    pub fn load_records(
   377	        &self,
   378	        package_name: &PackageName,

   388	            variant_consolidation,
   389	            base_url,
   390	            &self.channel,
   391	            &self.subdir,
   392	            self.patch_record_fn,
   393	            |_| true, // Dont filter anything out
   394	        )
   395	    }
   396	
   397	    /// Returns all the records for the specified package format(s).
   398	    pub fn load_all_records(
   399	        &self,
   400	        variant_consolidation: PackageFormatSelection,
   401	    ) -> io::Result<Vec<RepoDataRecord>> {
   402	        let repo_data = self.inner.borrow_repo_data();
   403	        let base_url = repo_data.info.as_ref().and_then(|i| i.base_url.as_deref());
   404	        parse_records(
   405	            None,
   406	            &repo_data.packages,
   407	            &repo_data.conda_packages,
   408	            &repo_data.experimental_v3,
   409	            variant_consolidation,
   410	            base_url,
   411	            &self.channel,
   412	            &self.subdir,
   413	            self.patch_record_fn,
   414	            |_| true,
   415	        )
   416	    }
   417	
   418	    /// Given a set of [`SparseRepoData`]s load all the records for the packages
   419	    /// with the specified names and all the packages these records depend
   420	    /// on.
   421	    ///
   422	    /// This will parse the records for the specified packages as well as all
   423	    /// the packages these records depend on.
   424	    pub fn load_records_recursive<'a>(
   425	        repo_data: impl IntoIterator<Item = &'a SparseRepoData>,
   426	        package_names: impl IntoIterator<Item = PackageName>,
   427	        patch_function: Option<fn(&mut PackageRecord)>,
   428	        variant_consolidation: PackageFormatSelection,
   429	    ) -> io::Result<Vec<Vec<RepoDataRecord>>> {
   430	        let repo_data: Vec<_> = repo_data.into_iter().collect();

OBSERVATION:

   602	fn parse_records<'i, F: Fn(&RepoDataRecord) -> bool>(
   603	    package_name: Option<&PackageName>,
   604	    tar_bz2_packages: &[(PackageFilename<'i>, &'i RawValue)],
   605	    conda_packages: &[(PackageFilename<'i>, &'i RawValue)],
   606	    v3: &LazyV3Packages<'i>,
   607	    variant_consolidation: PackageFormatSelection,
   608	    base_url: Option<&str>,
   609	    channel: &Channel,
   610	    subdir: &str,
   611	    patch_function: Option<fn(&mut PackageRecord)>,
   612	    filter_function: F,
   613	) -> io::Result<Vec<RepoDataRecord>> {
   614	    match variant_consolidation {
   615	        PackageFormatSelection::PreferConda => {
   616	            let tar_bz2 = add_stripped_filename(
   617	                find_package_in_slice(tar_bz2_packages, package_name, RecordKind::CondaOrTarBz2),
   618	                DistArchiveType::from(CondaArchiveType::TarBz2),
   619	            );
   620	            let v3_tar = add_stripped_filename(
   621	                find_package_in_slice(&v3.tar_bz2, package_name, RecordKind::V3TarBz2),
   622	                DistArchiveType::from(CondaArchiveType::TarBz2),
   623	            );
   624	            let all_tar = tar_bz2.merge_by(v3_tar, |(_, _, _, a), (_, _, _, b)| a <= b);
   625	
   626	            let conda = add_stripped_filename(
   627	                find_package_in_slice(conda_packages, package_name, RecordKind::CondaOrTarBz2),
   628	                DistArchiveType::from(CondaArchiveType::Conda),
   629	            );
   630	            let v3_conda_iter = add_stripped_filename(
   631	                find_package_in_slice(&v3.conda, package_name, RecordKind::V3Conda),
   632	                DistArchiveType::from(CondaArchiveType::Conda),
   633	            );
   634	            let all_conda = conda.merge_by(v3_conda_iter, |(_, _, _, a), (_, _, _, b)| a <= b);
   635	
   636	            let deduplicated_packages = all_conda
   637	                .merge_by(all_tar, |(_, _, _, left), (_, _, _, right)| left <= right)
   638	                .dedup_by(|(_, _, _, left), (_, _, _, right)| left == right)
   639	                .map(|(filename, raw_json, record_kind, _)| (filename, raw_json, record_kind));
   640	            parse_records_raw(
   641	                deduplicated_packages,
   642	                base_url,
   643	                channel,
   644	                subdir,
   645	                patch_function,
   646	                filter_function,
   647	            )
   648	        }
   649	        PackageFormatSelection::PreferCondaWithWhl => {
   650	            let tar_bz2 = add_stripped_filename(
   651	                find_package_in_slice(tar_bz2_packages, package_name, RecordKind::CondaOrTarBz2),
   652	                DistArchiveType::from(CondaArchiveType::TarBz2),
   653	            );
   654	            let v3_tar = add_stripped_filename(
   655	                find_package_in_slice(&v3.tar_bz2, package_name, RecordKind::V3TarBz2),
   656	                DistArchiveType::from(CondaArchiveType::TarBz2),
   657	            );
   658	            let all_tar = tar_bz2.merge_by(v3_tar, |(_, _, _, a), (_, _, _, b)| a <= b);
   659	
   660	            let whl = add_stripped_filename(
   661	                find_package_in_slice(&v3.whl, package_name, RecordKind::V3Whl),
   662	                DistArchiveType::from(WheelArchiveType::Whl),
   663	            );
   664	
   665	            let conda = add_stripped_filename(
   666	                find_package_in_slice(conda_packages, package_name, RecordKind::CondaOrTarBz2),
   667	                DistArchiveType::from(CondaArchiveType::Conda),
   668	            );
   669	            let v3_conda_iter = add_stripped_filename(
   670	                find_package_in_slice(&v3.conda, package_name, RecordKind::V3Conda),
   671	                DistArchiveType::from(CondaArchiveType::Conda),
   672	            );
   673	            let all_conda = conda.merge_by(v3_conda_iter, |(_, _, _, a), (_, _, _, b)| a <= b);
   674	
   675	            let deduplicated_packages = all_conda
   676	                .merge_by(whl, |(_, _, _, left), (_, _, _, right)| left <= right)
   677	                .merge_by(all_tar, |(_, _, _, left), (_, _, _, right)| left <= right)
   678	                .dedup_by(|(_, _, _, left), (_, _, _, right)| left == right)
   679	                .map(|(filename, raw_json, kind, _)| (filename, raw_json, kind));
   680	            parse_records_raw(
   681	                deduplicated_packages,
   682	                base_url,
   683	                channel,
   684	                subdir,
   685	                patch_function,
   686	                filter_function,
   687	            )
   688	        }
   689	        PackageFormatSelection::Both => {
   690	            let tar_bz2 =
   691	                find_package_in_slice(tar_bz2_packages, package_name, RecordKind::CondaOrTarBz2);
   692	            let conda =
   693	                find_package_in_slice(conda_packages, package_name, RecordKind::CondaOrTarBz2);
   694	            let v3_tar = find_package_in_slice(&v3.tar_bz2, package_name, RecordKind::V3TarBz2);
   695	            let v3_conda_iter = find_package_in_slice(&v3.conda, package_name, RecordKind::V3Conda);
   696	            parse_records_raw(
   697	                tar_bz2.chain(conda).chain(v3_tar).chain(v3_conda_iter),
   698	                base_url,
   699	                channel,
   700	                subdir,

OBSERVATION:

   898	fn parse_records_raw<'i, F: Fn(&RepoDataRecord) -> bool>(
   899	    packages: impl Iterator<Item = (PackageFilename<'i>, &'i RawValue, RecordKind)>,
   900	    base_url: Option<&str>,
   901	    channel: &Channel,
   902	    subdir: &str,
   903	    patch_function: Option<fn(&mut PackageRecord)>,
   904	    filter_function: F,
   905	) -> io::Result<Vec<RepoDataRecord>> {
   906	    let channel_name = channel.base_url.url().clone().redact().to_string();
   907	    packages
   908	        .map(move |record| {
   909	            parse_record_raw(
   910	                record,
   911	                base_url,
   912	                channel,
   913	                Some(channel_name.clone()),
   914	                subdir,
   915	                patch_function,
   916	            )
   917	        })
   918	        .filter_ok(filter_function)
   919	        .collect()
   920	}
   921	
   922	/// A helper function that immediately loads the records for the given packages
   923	/// (and their dependencies). Records for the specified packages are loaded from
   924	/// the repodata files. The `patch_record_fn` is applied to each record after it
   925	/// has been parsed and can mutate the record after it has been loaded.
   926	#[cfg(any(unix, windows))]
   927	pub async fn load_repo_data_recursively(
   928	    repo_data_paths: impl IntoIterator<Item = (Channel, impl Into<String>, impl AsRef<Path>)>,
   929	    package_names: impl IntoIterator<Item = PackageName>,
   930	    patch_function: Option<fn(&mut PackageRecord)>,
   931	    variant_consolidation: PackageFormatSelection,
   932	) -> Result<Vec<Vec<RepoDataRecord>>, io::Error> {
   933	    use futures::{StreamExt, TryFutureExt, TryStreamExt};
   934	
   935	    // Open the different files and memory map them to get access to their bytes. Do
   936	    // this in parallel.
   937	    let lazy_repo_data = futures::stream::iter(repo_data_paths)
   938	        .map(|(channel, subdir, path)| {
   939	            let path = path.as_ref().to_path_buf();
   940	            let subdir = subdir.into();
   941	            tokio::task::spawn_blocking(move || {
   942	                SparseRepoData::from_file(channel, subdir, path, patch_function)
   943	            })
   944	            .unwrap_or_else(|r| match r.try_into_panic() {
   945	                Ok(panic) => std::panic::resume_unwind(panic),
   946	                Err(err) => Err(io::Error::other(err.to_string())),
   947	            })
   948	        })
   949	        .buffered(50)
   950	        .try_collect::<Vec<_>>()

OBSERVATION:

   100	    /// This method ensures that if the channel is a url, it does not end with a
   101	    /// `/`.
   102	    pub fn as_str(&self) -> &str {
   103	        match self {
   104	            NamedChannelOrUrl::Name(name) => name,
   105	            NamedChannelOrUrl::Url(url) => url.as_str().trim_end_matches('/'),
   106	            NamedChannelOrUrl::Path(path) => path.as_str(),
   107	        }
   108	    }
   109	
   110	    /// Converts the channel to a base url using the given configuration.
   111	    /// This method ensures that the base url always ends with a `/`.
   112	    pub fn into_base_url(self, config: &ChannelConfig) -> Result<ChannelUrl, ParseChannelError> {
   113	        let url = match self {
   114	            NamedChannelOrUrl::Name(name) => {
   115	                let mut base_url = config.channel_alias.clone();
   116	                if let Ok(mut segments) = base_url.path_segments_mut() {
   117	                    for segment in name.split(&['/', '\\']) {
   118	                        segments.push(segment);
   119	                    }
   120	                }
   121	                base_url.into()
   122	            }
   123	            NamedChannelOrUrl::Url(url) => url.into(),
   124	            NamedChannelOrUrl::Path(path) => {
   125	                let absolute_path = absolute_path(path.as_str(), &config.root_dir)?;
   126	                directory_path_to_url(absolute_path.to_path())
   127	                    .map_err(|_err| ParseChannelError::InvalidPath(path.to_string()))?
   128	                    .into()
   129	            }
   130	        };
   131	        Ok(url)
   132	    }
   133	
   134	    /// Converts this instance into a channel.
   135	    pub fn into_channel(self, config: &ChannelConfig) -> Result<Channel, ParseChannelError> {
   136	        let name = match &self {
   137	            NamedChannelOrUrl::Name(name) => Some(name.clone()),
   138	            NamedChannelOrUrl::Url(base_url) => config.strip_channel_alias(base_url),
   139	            NamedChannelOrUrl::Path(_) => None,
   140	        };
   141	        let base_url = self.into_base_url(config)?;
   142	        Ok(Channel {
   143	            name,
   144	            base_url,
   145	            platforms: None,
   146	        })
   147	    }
   148	}
   149	
   150	impl Display for NamedChannelOrUrl {

     1	use std::fmt::{Debug, Display, Formatter};
     2	
     3	use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};
     4	use url::Url;
     5	
     6	use crate::{utils::url_with_trailing_slash::UrlWithTrailingSlash, Platform};
     7	
     8	/// Represents a channel base url. This is a wrapper around an url that is
     9	/// normalized:
    10	///
    11	/// * The URL always contains a trailing `/`.
    12	///
    13	/// This is useful to be able to compare different channels.
    14	#[derive(Clone, Hash, Eq, PartialEq, Deserialize)]
    15	#[serde(transparent)]
    16	pub struct ChannelUrl(UrlWithTrailingSlash);
    17	
    18	impl ChannelUrl {
    19	    /// Returns the base Url of the channel.
    20	    pub fn url(&self) -> &UrlWithTrailingSlash {
    21	        &self.0
    22	    }
    23	
    24	    /// Returns the string representation of the url.
    25	    pub fn as_str(&self) -> &str {
    26	        self.0.as_str()
    27	    }
    28	
    29	    /// Append the platform to the base url.
    30	    pub fn platform_url(&self, platform: Platform) -> Url {
    31	        self.0
    32	            .join(&format!("{}/", platform.as_str())) // trailing slash is important here as this signifies a directory
    33	            .expect("platform is a valid url fragment")
    34	    }
    35	}
    36	
    37	// Override the behavior of the `Serialize` trait to remove the trailing slash.
    38	// In code, we want to ensure that a trailing slash is always present but when
    39	// we serialize the type it can be removed to safe space and make it look better
    40	// for humans.
    41	impl Serialize for ChannelUrl {
    42	    fn serialize<S>(&self, serializer: S) -> Result<S::Ok, S::Error>
    43	    where
    44	        S: serde::Serializer,
    45	    {
    46	        self.as_str().trim_end_matches('/').serialize(serializer)
    47	    }
    48	}
    49	
    50	impl Debug for ChannelUrl {
    51	    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result {
    52	        write!(f, "{}", self.0.as_str())
    53	    }
    54	}
    55	
    56	impl From<Url> for ChannelUrl {
    57	    fn from(url: Url) -> Self {
    58	        Self(UrlWithTrailingSlash::from(url))
    59	    }
    60	}
    61	
    62	impl From<ChannelUrl> for Url {
    63	    fn from(value: ChannelUrl) -> Self {
    64	        value.0.into()
    65	    }
    66	}
    67	
    68	impl AsRef<Url> for ChannelUrl {
    69	    fn as_ref(&self) -> &Url {
    70	        &self.0
    71	    }
    72	}
    73	
    74	impl Display for ChannelUrl {
    75	    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result {
    76	        write!(f, "{}", &self.0)
    77	    }
    78	}

   265

   271	        // Case 3: migrated_channel_aliases
   272	        // Case 4: custom_channels matches
   273	        // Case 5: channel_alias match
   274

   276	            // Case 7: Fallback

So `Channel::from_url` takes a `ChannelUrl` and creates a `Channel` with a `name` derived from the path. The `name` would be something like `"conda-forge"` for `https://conda.anaconda.org/conda-forge`, or `"github-releases"` for `https://prefix.dev/github-releases`.

But the repo data record's `channel` field is set to the redacted base URL (from `channel.base_url.url().clone().redact().to_string()`). So it's the full URL, not the short name.

Let me now verify my theory about the mismatch. Let me write a small script to understand what's happening.

Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the code in `features_ext.rs` more carefully. The `prioritized_channels()` method returns channels with their `PrioritizedChannel` metadata. The `channel.channel` is a `NamedChannelOrUrl`. For the channel config `{ channel = "https://prefix.dev/github-releases", exclude-newer = "2d" }`, this would be `NamedChannelOrUrl::Url("https://prefix.dev/github-releases")`.

Then in `exclude_newer_config_resolved`:
```rust
*config = config.clone().with_channel_cutoff(
    channel.channel.to_string(),  // "https://prefix.dev/github-releases"
    ExcludeNewer::Duration(duration).cutoff(),  // now - 2d
);
```

So the key is `"https://prefix.dev/github-releases"` (no trailing slash).

But when the repo data is fetched, the channel URL would be `"https://prefix.dev/github-releases/"` (with trailing slash). So the match would fail.

However, I'm not 100% sure this is the issue described in the PR. The PR says the workspace-level `exclude-newer` overrides the channel version. Let me think about this differently.

Actually, re-reading the PR description:
> It should be `workspace.exclude-newer` > `workspace.channels.exclude-newer` > `[(pypi-)exclude-newer]` IMO.

The author is saying the precedence should be:
1. `workspace.exclude-newer` is the default/fallback
2. `workspace.channels.exclude-newer` overrides the default for specific channels
3. `[(pypi-)exclude-newer]` (per-package overrides) have the highest precedence

This is what the code already does, except the keys might not match. 

But wait, let me re-read the error message:
```
chainsaw * cannot be installed because there are no viable options:
   └─ chainsaw 0.2.5 | 0.2.6 | ... is excluded because the package is uploaded after the cutoff date of 2025-04-08 04:56:48.943953
```

The cutoff date is `2025-04-08`. If the workspace `exclude-newer = "1y"` and today is April 2026, then `now - 1y` ≈ April 2025. This matches! So the workspace-level `1y` is being applied.

But if the channel-level `2d` was correctly applied, the cutoff would be `now - 2d` ≈ April 2026, which would include packages from the last 2 days. The package `chainsaw` has versions up to 0.2.14, which might have been released recently.

So the channel-level cutoff is not being applied. The question is: why?

Could it be that the channel-level cutoff is correctly set in the config, but the channel name in the repo data doesn't match? Let me verify.

The issue is that if there are MULTIPLE channels with `exclude_newer`, each iteration creates a new config from the previous one, adding one channel cutoff at a time. This should work correctly.\n\nBut wait: the first line sets `exclude_newer` to `Some(ResolvedExcludeNewer::from_datetime(workspace_cutoff))`. Then for each channel, it gets or inserts into this `Option`. Since it\'s already `Some`, `get_or_insert_with` returns the existing config. Then it clones it, adds a channel cutoff, and assigns back. This should preserve previous channel cutoffs.\n\nSo the logic seems correct for building the config. The issue must be the key mismatch.\n\nBut hold on, let me check if there\'s another issue. Let me check how `NamedChannelOrUrl` parses this:\n\n```rust\nfn from_str(s: &str) -> Result<Self, Self::Err> {\n    if parse_scheme(s).is_some() {\n        Ok(NamedChannelOrUrl::Url(Url::from_str(s)?))\n    } else if is_path(s) {\n        Ok(NamedChannelOrUrl::Path(s.into()))\n    } else {\n        Ok(NamedChannelOrUrl::Name(s.to_string()))\n    }\n}\n```\n\nSince `"https://prefix.dev/github-releases"` has a scheme (`https`), it\'s parsed as `NamedChannelOrUrl::Url(...)`. Now I see the flow clearly. The `exclude_newer` (as `rattler_solve::ExcludeNewer`) is passed to the solver task. The solver then calls `is_excluded` on each record with the record's channel.

Now, the question is: does the channel string in the repo data record match the channel key in `channel_cutoffs`?

For the PR example:
- Channel config: `{ channel = "https://prefix.dev/github-releases", exclude-newer = "2d" }`
- `NamedChannelOrUrl::Url("https://prefix.dev/github-releases")`
- `to_string()` = `"https://prefix.dev/github-releases"` (no trailing slash)
- `into_base_url(config)` = `ChannelUrl("https://prefix.dev/github-releases/")` (with trailing slash)

So the channel cutoff key is `"https://prefix.dev/github-releases"` but the repo data record's channel is `"https://prefix.dev/github-releases/"`.

This mismatch would cause the channel-level cutoff to never be matched, falling back to the default workspace-level cutoff.

But wait, what about `NamedChannelOrUrl::Name("conda-forge")`?
- `to_string()` = `"conda-forge"`
- `into_base_url(config)` = `ChannelUrl("https://conda.anaconda.org/conda-forge/")`

The channel cutoff key would be `"conda-forge"` but the repo data record's channel would be `"https://conda.anaconda.org/conda-forge/"`. These also don't match!

So channel-level exclude-newer would NEVER work for any channel, whether named or URL. But the tests pass because they test `cutoff_for_package` directly with the short name.

Now let me verify this by looking at the tests in `test_exclude_newer_package_channel_workspace_precedence`:

```rust
[workspace]
channels = [{ channel = "my-private-forge", exclude-newer = "2016-12-02T02:07:43Z" }, "conda-forge"]
exclude-newer = "2015-12-02T02:07:43Z"
...
let private_numpy_cutoff = config.cutoff_for_package(&numpy, Some("my-private-forge"));
assert_eq!(private_numpy_cutoff, channel_cutoff);
```

This test calls `cutoff_for_package` with `Some("my-private-forge")` directly. But in the real solver, it would pass the repo data record's channel which would be the full URL `"https://conda.anaconda.org/my-private-forge"` (or some custom channel alias). So the test doesn't cover the actual matching.

So the fix should be to use the channel's base URL (the `ChannelUrl`) as the key instead of the `NamedChannelOrUrl`'s string representation. Or alternatively, we need to ensure the keys match by normalizing them similarly.

But wait, let me check how the repo data record's channel matches the channel URL. The channel URL passed to the gateway is `ChannelUrl`. The sparse repo data stores a `Channel`. The `channel_name` in `parse_records_raw` is `channel.base_url.url().clone().redact().to_string()`. 

So the channel_name is the `ChannelUrl` as a string (with trailing slash). The `ChannelUrl` is derived from `NamedChannelOrUrl::into_base_url()`.

So the key in `channel_cutoffs` should be the `ChannelUrl` string (with trailing slash), but currently it's the `NamedChannelOrUrl::to_string()` (without trailing slash for URLs, and short name for named channels).

Now, there's an even bigger issue: for named channels like `"conda-forge"`, the `NamedChannelOrUrl::to_string()` returns `"conda-forge"`, but the repo data record's channel would be `"https://conda.anaconda.org/conda-forge/"`. These are completely different strings!

So the fix needs to:
1. Use `ChannelUrl` (the base URL) as the key for channel cutoffs, not `NamedChannelOrUrl::to_string()`

But wait, in `exclude_newer_config_resolved`, we don't have access to the channel config at that point. The function is on `FeaturesExt` which doesn't have the channel config.

Hmm, but actually we could look at how the channels are converted elsewhere. Let me look at the `SolveCondaEnvironmentSpec` building:

```rust
let channels = group.channels().into_iter().cloned().collect_vec();
...
let channels = channels
    .iter()
    .map(|c| c.clone().into_base_url(&channel_config))
    .collect::<Result<Vec<_>, _>>()?;
```

So the channels are `NamedChannelOrUrl` and they get converted to `ChannelUrl` via `into_base_url`. The `exclude_newer_config_resolved` runs on the group (which implements `FeaturesExt`), and it doesn't have access to `channel_config`.

But wait, `exclude_newer_config` takes `&self` and returns `Result<Option<rattler_solve::ExcludeNewer>, ParseChannelError>`. It doesn't take a `ChannelConfig`. But the conversion from `ResolvedExcludeNewer` to `rattler_solve::ExcludeNewer` doesn't need a `ChannelConfig`. The issue is just the key mismatch.

OK so the fix should be in `exclude_newer_config_resolved`. Instead of using `channel.channel.to_string()` as the key, we should use something that matches what the repo data record will have. But we don't have the `ChannelConfig` at this point.

However, looking at the code flow:
1. `group.exclude_newer_config_resolved()` is called to get `ResolvedExcludeNewer`
2. Then `channels` are converted to `ChannelUrl` via `into_base_url(&channel_config)`
3. The `ResolvedExcludeNewer` is passed to the solver along with the `ChannelUrl`s

But wait, the `ResolvedExcludeNewer` is converted to `rattler_solve::ExcludeNewer` via `From` impl which just passes through the channel cutoffs as-is. Then the solver uses `record.channel` to match against these keys.

So the issue is that the keys set in step 1 don't match what `record.channel` will be in step 3.

The fix should ensure that the keys used in `channel_cutoffs` match the `record.channel` values. The `record.channel` is the `ChannelUrl` as a string (with trailing slash, redacted).

So we need to use `ChannelUrl` strings as keys. But `exclude_newer_config_resolved` doesn't have access to `ChannelConfig`. However, we could pass the `ChannelConfig` or we could compute the `ChannelUrl` from the `NamedChannelOrUrl` directly.

Actually, looking at `prioritized_channels()`:
```rust
fn prioritized_channels(
    &self,
) -> IndexMap<&'source NamedChannelOrUrl, &'source PrioritizedChannel> {
```

We have `NamedChannelOrUrl` references. But we need `ChannelUrl` to match what the repo data records will have.

Hmm, but looking at the PR description more carefully: the author says the workspace-level `exclude-newer` overrides the channel-level one. Let me check if maybe the issue is different - maybe the channel-level `exclude-newer` is simply being ignored entirely because the code path doesn't even process it correctly.

Let me re-read the `exclude_newer_config_resolved` function one more time:

```rust
fn exclude_newer_config_resolved(
    &self,
) -> Result<Option<ResolvedExcludeNewer>, ParseChannelError> {
    let mut exclude_newer = self
        .exclude_newer_raw()
        .map(|config| ResolvedExcludeNewer::from_datetime(config.cutoff()));

    for channel in self.prioritized_channels().into_values() {
        let Some(channel_exclude_newer) = channel.exclude_newer else {
            continue;
        };

        let config = exclude_newer.get_or_insert_with(|| {
            ResolvedExcludeNewer::from_datetime(DateTime::<Utc>::MAX_UTC)
        });

        *config = match channel_exclude_newer {
            ExcludeNewer::Timestamp(dt) => config
                .clone()
                .with_channel_cutoff(channel.channel.to_string(), dt),
            ExcludeNewer::Duration(duration) => config.clone().with_channel_cutoff(
                channel.channel.to_string(),
                ExcludeNewer::Duration(duration).cutoff(),
            ),
        };
    }

    for (name, package_exclude_newer) in &self
        .workspace_manifest()
        .workspace
        .exclude_newer_package_overrides
    {
        let config = exclude_newer.get_or_insert_with(|| {
            ResolvedExcludeNewer::from_datetime(DateTime::<Utc>::MAX_UTC)
        });

        *config = match package_exclude_newer {
            ExcludeNewer::Timestamp(dt) => {
                config.clone().with_package_cutoff(name.clone(), *dt)
            }
            ExcludeNewer::Duration(duration) => config
                .clone()
                .with_package_cutoff(name.clone(), ExcludeNewer::Duration(*duration).cutoff()),
        };
    }

    Ok(exclude_newer)
}
```

I see the problem now! On line 140, when applying a channel override, the code does:
```rust
*config = match channel_exclude_newer {
    ExcludeNewer::Timestamp(dt) => config
        .clone()
        .with_channel_cutoff(channel.channel.to_string(), dt),
    ExcludeNewer::Duration(duration) => config.clone().with_channel_cutoff(
        channel.channel.to_string(),
        ExcludeNewer::Duration(duration).cutoff(),
    ),
};
```

This creates a NEW config (via `config.clone()`) with the channel cutoff added, and assigns it back to `config`. The issue is that if there are MULTIPLE channels with `exclude_newer`, each iteration creates a new config from the previous one, adding one channel cutoff at a time. This should work correctly.

But wait: the first line sets `exclude_newer` to `Some(ResolvedExcludeNewer::from_datetime(workspace_cutoff))`. Then for each channel, it gets or inserts into this `Option`. Since it's already `Some`, `get_or_insert_with` returns the existing config. Then it clones it, adds a channel cutoff, and assigns back. This should preserve previous channel cutoffs.

So the logic seems correct for building the config. The issue must be the key mismatch.

But hold on, let me check if there's another issue. The PR description says:

> On current unreleased main:
> ```toml
> [workspace]
> channels = [
>     { channel = "conda-forge", exclude-newer = "3y"},
>     { channel = "bioconda", exclude-newer = "1y"},
>     { channel = "https://prefix.dev/github-releases", exclude-newer = "2d"}]
> ```

Notice that the channel `"https://prefix.dev/github-releases"` is a URL. Let me check how `NamedChannelOrUrl` parses this:

```rust
fn from_str(s: &str) -> Result<Self, Self::Err> {
    if parse_scheme(s).is_some() {
        Ok(NamedChannelOrUrl::Url(Url::from_str(s)?))
    } else if is_path(s) {
        Ok(NamedChannelOrUrl::Path(s.into()))
    } else {
        Ok(NamedChannelOrUrl::Name(s.to_string()))
    }
}
```

Since `"https://prefix.dev/github-releases"` has a scheme (`https`), it's parsed as `NamedChannelOrUrl::Url(...)`. Good.

Now, `NamedChannelOrUrl::as_str()`:
```rust
pub fn as_str(&self) -> &str {
    match self {
        NamedChannelOrUrl::Name(name) => name,
        NamedChannelOrUrl::Url(url) => url.as_str().trim_end_matches('/'),
        NamedChannelOrUrl::Path(path) => path.as_str(),
    }
}
```

So for a URL, it trims trailing slashes. `"https://prefix.dev/github-releases"` has no trailing slash, so it stays as is.

`Display::fmt` calls `self.as_str()`, so `to_string()` returns `"https://prefix.dev/github-releases"`.

Now, `into_base_url`:
```rust
pub fn into_base_url(self, config: &ChannelConfig) -> Result<ChannelUrl, ParseChannelError> {
    let url = match self {
        NamedChannelOrUrl::Name(name) => { ... }
        NamedChannelOrUrl::Url(url) => url.into(),
        ...
    };
    Ok(url)
}
```

`Url::into()` for `ChannelUrl` goes through `From<Url> for ChannelUrl`:
```rust
impl From<Url> for ChannelUrl {
    fn from(url: Url) -> Self {
        Self(UrlWithTrailingSlash::from(url))
    }
}
```

Which adds a trailing slash. So the `ChannelUrl` would be `"https://prefix.dev/github-releases/"`.

Thus:
- Key in `channel_cutoffs`: `"https://prefix.dev/github-releases"` (from `to_string()`)
- Record channel: `"https://prefix.dev/github-releases/"` (from `ChannelUrl`)

These don't match!

And for named channels:
- Key in `channel_cutoffs`: `"conda-forge"` (from `to_string()`)
- Record channel: `"https://conda.anaconda.org/conda-forge/"` (from `ChannelUrl`)

These definitely don't match!

So the fix should be: instead of using `channel.channel.to_string()` as the key, we should use the `ChannelUrl` representation (i.e., the base URL). But we need access to `ChannelConfig` to convert `NamedChannelOrUrl` to `ChannelUrl`.

Let me check if `FeaturesExt` has access to `ChannelConfig`:

```rust

```

Actually, let me re-think. The PR says the issue is that workspace-level `exclude-newer` takes precedence over channel-level. Let me look at the code one more time with fresh eyes.

Wait, I just realized something. Let me look at the `prioritized_channels` method again:

```rust
fn prioritized_channels(
    &self,
) -> IndexMap<&'source NamedChannelOrUrl, &'source PrioritizedChannel> {
    let channels = self.features().flat_map(|feature| match &feature.channels {
        Some(channels) => channels,
        None => &self.workspace_manifest().workspace.channels,
    });

    let mut prioritized = IndexMap::new();
    for channel in channels.into_iter().sorted_by(|a, b| {
        let a = a.priority.unwrap_or(0);
        let b = b.priority.unwrap_or(0);
        b.cmp(&a)
    }) {
        prioritized.entry(&channel.channel).or_insert(channel);
    }

    prioritized
}
```

This deduplicates channels by `NamedChannelOrUrl`. The first channel (sorted by priority) wins. If there are multiple features with the same channel but different `exclude_newer` settings, only the first one's `exclude_newer` is kept.

But the PR example has channels defined only in `[workspace]`, not in features, so this shouldn't be an issue.

2026-05-30 18:46:18,956 - INFO - swea-agent-prefix-dev__pixi-5850 - 💭 THOUGHT
Now let me look at where the `channel_cutoffs` are actually used to match against repo data records. The key usage is in `rattler_solve::ExcludeNewer::cutoff_for_package`:

```rust
pub fn cutoff_for_package(
    &self,
    package: &PackageName,
    channel: Option<&str>,
) -> DateTime<Utc> {
    self.package_cutoffs
        .get(package)
        .copied()
        .or_else(|| channel.and_then(|channel| self.channel_cutoffs.get(channel).copied()))
        .unwrap_or(self.cutoff)
}
```

So the channel from the repo data record is used as a key to look up in `channel_cutoffs`. The issue is that the keys in `channel_cutoffs` are `NamedChannelOrUrl::to_string()` values, while the keys used for lookup are `RepoDataRecord::channel` (which is the `ChannelUrl` string).

Now I'm confident about the fix. We need to use the `ChannelUrl` (base URL) as the key in `channel_cutoffs`, not the `NamedChannelOrUrl` string.

But wait, `exclude_newer_config_resolved` doesn't have access to `ChannelConfig`. We need to either:
1. Add `ChannelConfig` as a parameter
2. Compute the base URL ourselves

Let me check if we can get the `ChannelConfig` from the workspace manifest:

So `channel_config` is available at the call site. But the function `exclude_newer_config_resolved` is defined on `FeaturesExt` trait, which doesn't have access to `ChannelConfig`.

I think the cleanest fix would be to change `exclude_newer_config_resolved` to accept a `&ChannelConfig` parameter. But this would be a bigger change.

Alternatively, we could fix the key to use the `ChannelUrl` representation. Let me think about what the `ChannelUrl` would be for each `NamedChannelOrUrl` variant:

1. `NamedChannelOrUrl::Name("conda-forge")` → `ChannelUrl` = `"https://conda.anaconda.org/conda-forge/"` (using default channel alias)
2. `NamedChannelOrUrl::Url("https://prefix.dev/github-releases")` → `ChannelUrl` = `"https://prefix.dev/github-releases/"` (just adding trailing slash)
3. `NamedChannelOrUrl::Path("/some/path")` → `ChannelUrl` = `"file:///some/path/"` (directory URL)

So we need `ChannelConfig` to properly compute these.

But wait, let me look at how the `RepoDataRecord::channel` is set again:

```rust
let channel_name = channel.base_url.url().clone().redact().to_string();
```

`channel.base_url` is a `ChannelUrl`. `ChannelUrl::url()` returns `&UrlWithTrailingSlash`. The `.redact()` method redacts sensitive information (like passwords). The `.to_string()` on the redacted URL would return the URL as a string with trailing slash.

So the record's channel is the redacted base URL string.

Now, for the channel cutoff key, we should also use the redacted base URL string. But we need the `ChannelConfig` to convert a `NamedChannelOrUrl` to a `ChannelUrl`.

However, maybe there's a simpler approach: we could use `NamedChannelOrUrl::into_base_url` ourselves, but that requires `ChannelConfig`. Let me check if we can get `ChannelConfig` from the workspace.

Let me check what `group.workspace()` returns:

   138	pub struct Workspace {
   139	    /// Root folder of the workspace
   140	    root: PathBuf,
   141	
   142	    /// The name of the workspace based on the location of the workspace.
   143	    /// This is used to determine the name of the workspace when no name is
   144	    /// specified.
   145	    manifest_location_name: Option<String>,
   146	
   147	    /// Reqwest client shared for this workspace.
   148	    /// This is wrapped in a `OnceLock` to allow for lazy initialization.
   149	    // TODO: once https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/109737 is stabilized, switch to OnceLock
   150	    client: OnceCell<(LazyReqwestClient, rattler_networking::LazyClient)>,
   151	
   152	    /// The repodata gateway to use for answering queries about repodata.
   153	    /// This is wrapped in a `OnceLock` to allow for lazy initialization.
   154	    // TODO: once https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/109737 is stabilized, switch to OnceLock
   155	    repodata_gateway: OnceCell<Gateway>,
   156	
   157	    /// The manifest for the workspace
   158	    pub workspace: WithProvenance<WorkspaceManifest>,
   159	
   160	    /// The manifest of the "current" package. This is the package from which
   161	    /// the workspace was discovered. This might be `None` if no package was
   162	    /// discovered on the current path.
   163	    pub package: Option<WithProvenance<PackageManifest>>,
   164	
   165	    /// The environment variables that are activated when the environment is
   166	    /// activated. Cached per environment, for both clean and normal
   167	    env_vars: HashMap<EnvironmentName, EnvironmentVars>,
   168	
   169	    /// The cache that contains mapping
   170	    mapping_source: OnceCell<MappingSource>,
   171	
   172	    /// The global configuration as loaded from the config file(s)
   173	    config: Config,
   174	
   175	    /// The S3 configuration

   177

   180
